perturbation.original_text: The Company agrees that, concurrent with the appointment of the New Directors to the Board, the Board shall take such action as is necessary such that each of the New Directors is appointed to at least one (1) of the three (3) standing committees of the Board that the Company is required to maintain in accordance with the NASDAQ listing standards; provided that, with respect to each such committee appointment, the New Director is and continues to remain eligible to serve as a member of such committee pursuant to applicable law and the rules of NASDAQ that are applicable to the composition of such committee.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company agrees that, concurrent with the appointment of the New Directors to the Board, the Board will consider taking action such that the New Directors may potentially be appointed to at least one of the three standing committees, if deemed suitable. Eligibility will be assessed at the Board's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation by changing "shall take such action as is necessary such that each of the New Directors is appointed" to "will consider taking action such that the New Directors may potentially be appointed". Adding "if deemed suitable" and "Eligibility will be assessed at the Board's discretion" makes the appointment non-mandatory and subject to the Board's subjective assessment, which contradicts the initial intention of ensuring the New Directors have influence through committee membership.
perturbation.law_explanation: 8 Del. C. § 141(a) states that the business and affairs of a corporation shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. By making the appointment of new directors to committees discretionary rather than mandatory, the modified text could be interpreted as an abdication of the Board's responsibility to actively manage the corporation by integrating new perspectives into committee work. It could be argued that the board is not actively engaging in management of the company by not taking action to integrate the new directors, potentially violating their duty under Delaware law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation of appointing new directors to committees. The original text stated the board "shall take such action as is necessary such that each of the New Directors is appointed," while the changed text says the board "will consider taking action such that the New Directors may potentially be appointed, if deemed suitable." This introduces ambiguity and discretion where there was originally a clear obligation. The Law Explanation suggests this could be a dereliction of duty under 8 Del. C. § 141(a). Scraped Law Snippet 1, which quotes 8 Del. C. § 141, states that "the business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors..." By making the appointment discretionary, the modified text arguably contradicts this requirement for the board to actively manage the company by integrating new directors into committees, creating a contradiction. The change from 'shall take such action' to 'will consider taking action' directly weakens the obligation to a point where it is not mandated, violating the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Investor Group acknowledges that each of the New Directors shall be required to comply with all policies, processes, procedures, codes, rules, standards, and guidelines applicable to members of the Board, as in effect from time to time, including, but not limited to, the Company's Code of Conduct, and policies on confidentiality, ethics, hedging and pledging of Company securities, public disclosures, stock trading, and stock ownership, and that each of the New Directors shall be required to strictly preserve the confidentiality of Company business and information, including the discussion of any matters considered in meetings of the Board whether or not the matters relate to material non-public information, unless previously publicly disclosed by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Investor Group acknowledges that each of the New Directors will generally adhere to the Company's policies and guidelines for Board members, including those related to confidentiality and ethics, unless extenuating circumstances arise.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates strict compliance with all Board policies. The modified text replaces "shall be required to comply" with "will generally adhere to", which introduces ambiguity and makes compliance discretionary. The addition of "unless extenuating circumstances arise" provides an undefined exception, further weakening the obligation and opening it to interpretation. This can lead to a situation where a director might claim an 'extenuating circumstance' to justify non-compliance, potentially violating insider trading laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 194 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The original text emphasizes strict confidentiality, which is crucial for preventing insider trading. By allowing 'extenuating circumstances' to justify non-adherence to confidentiality policies, the modified text could create a loophole that makes it more difficult to enforce insider trading regulations. Directors might disclose material non-public information under the guise of 'extenuating circumstances,' thereby violating the Act.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the requirement for new directors to comply with company policies, potentially contradicting Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5, which prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The original text requires strict compliance, while the changed text introduces ambiguity by stating that directors will 'generally adhere' to policies, 'unless extenuating circumstances arise.' This creates a loophole where directors might disclose material non-public information under the guise of 'extenuating circumstances,' violating insider trading regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 78j, addresses 'manipulative and deceptive devices,' which aligns with the concern that weakening confidentiality requirements could facilitate illegal activities. The change from 'shall be required to comply' to 'will generally adhere' significantly reduces the enforceability of company policies designed to prevent insider trading, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the Company and the Investors shall be responsible for its own fees and expenses incurred in connection with the negotiation, execution, and effectuation of this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby, including, but not limited to attorneys' fees incurred in connection with the negotiation and execution of this Agreement and all other activities related to the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company and the Investors will typically bear their own costs related to this Agreement; however, certain shared expenses may be subject to further negotiation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states each party is responsible for their own fees and expenses. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating "will typically bear their own costs" and adding "certain shared expenses may be subject to further negotiation." This makes the allocation of expenses uncertain and potentially open to disputes. It could contradict generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) if it's unclear who is responsible for specific expenses, affecting the financial reporting of both parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 11 of the Securities Act of 1933 imposes liability for false or misleading statements in a registration statement. If the allocation of expenses is not clearly defined and subsequently misreported, this could lead to inaccuracies in financial statements filed with the SEC. The phrase "certain shared expenses may be subject to further negotiation" creates an ambiguity regarding who ultimately bears the cost. If this is not accurately reflected in the financial statements, it could be considered a material misstatement, potentially violating Section 11 of the Securities Act.

Civil liabilities on account of false registration statement (a) Persons possessing cause of action; persons liable In case any part of the registration statement, when such part became effective, contained an untrue statement of a material fact or omitted to state a material fact required to be stated therein or necessary to make the state
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding who bears the costs, potentially leading to misreporting of expenses in financial statements. The original text clearly assigns responsibility for fees and expenses, while the modified text introduces uncertainty, contradicting the need for clear financial reporting. Snippet 1, 15 USC 77k, discusses civil liabilities for false registration statements due to untrue statements of material fact or omissions. If the ambiguity in the modified text results in inaccurate financial reporting related to these expenses, it could violate Section 11 of the Securities Act of 1933, as mentioned in the Law Explanation. This aligns with the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition, where ambiguity in a legal statement leads to difficulties in enforcing obligations under law, potentially causing non-compliance.
